questions.question: What is the main reason to move slow work onto a message queue instead of doing it in the request?
options: It makes the slow work itself run faster
It decouples the fast response from the slow work, so the user gets an instant reply and the work happens in the background
It removes the need for any workers
It guarantees the work never fails
questions.explanation: A queue lets the producer accept the request and return immediately while workers do the slow work later. The work isn't faster — it's moved off the critical path — so the user isn't stuck waiting and servers aren't tied up holding requests.

questions.question: How does a queue help during a sudden traffic spike?
options: It drops the excess requests
It buffers the excess work so arrival rate and processing rate are independent; workers drain the backlog steadily
It makes the workers process infinitely fast
It rejects all requests until the spike ends
questions.explanation: The queue acts as a shock absorber: a burst of 10,000 jobs is accepted instantly and buffered, then drained at the workers' steady rate. Without it, the spike would overwhelm servers and drop work. The trade is that buffered jobs wait a bit longer.

questions.question: What does a steadily growing queue depth indicate?
options: The system is perfectly healthy
Arrivals are exceeding processing capacity — you're falling behind and need more workers or to shed load
The queue is about to make jobs faster
Workers are processing too quickly
questions.explanation: Queue depth is a health signal. Near zero means workers keep up. Steadily growing means the arrival rate exceeds processing capacity; left unchecked the queue grows unbounded until it exhausts memory/disk. The fix is more workers or backpressure — not a bigger queue.

questions.question: A worker crashes mid-job before acknowledging the message. What typically happens?
options: The message is lost forever
The unacknowledged message is redelivered to another worker, at the cost of possible duplicate processing
The entire queue is deleted
The producer is notified to recompute it
questions.explanation: Workers ack only after success. If a worker dies before acking, the message reappears (after a visibility timeout) and another worker processes it — so work isn't lost. The tradeoff is that the job might run twice, which is why consumers should be idempotent (Lesson 05).

questions.question: Which kind of work is a queue NOT suitable for?
options: Sending a confirmation email after signup
Generating a thumbnail for an uploaded image
Computing the exact page content the user is waiting to see in this response
Recording analytics events
questions.explanation: Queues fit asynchronous work whose result the user doesn't need right now (emails, thumbnails, analytics). You can't queue work whose output must be in the current response — the user is waiting for it, so it has to happen synchronously.
